title: Bernie's plan sucks, actually
transcript: So, a lot of you have asked me to cover Bernie Sanders' proposed AI sovereign wealth fund, where the most alarming number from that proposal is that he wants to capture 50% of AI companies. So, let's get into it. I've done a lot of research on this topic, and this is honestly what I've been focusing on for the last 2 years as I've been working on post-labor economics and Labor Zero. So, here's my analysis. So, the first thing that you need to understand about this particular proposal is that it's technically a kind of windfall tax. So, when Bernie Sanders says that he wants to capture 50% of OpenAI and Anthropic and xAI, it's basically just a one-time expropriation of wealth. Now, that If that sounds alarming to you, then then good, because it is alarming. So, let's unpack it. The whole thing was blown wide open on June 1st via a New York Times op-ed. So, this was not actual legislation. This was going straight to the media. The mechanism is a one-time 50% tax paid in company stock. Um so, that's equity, not cash or profits. They want a controlling stake in companies such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI. And the vehicle would be a newly created federal sovereign wealth fund. Now, in principle, I am not opposed to sovereign wealth funds. In fact, this is one of the things that I am very much in favor of. And in fact, I think we should have multiple wealth funds at very various levels of government. We should have federal wealth funds. Uh we should have multiple of them. We should have state wealth funds and also municipal and local wealth funds. However, this isn't really how you do it. So, the moral foundation of this whole argument, the reason that he says that we should we should take 50% is generative AI models are trained on humanity's collective knowledge, creative work, and data without compensation. And also, a lot of the research that went into it was publicly funded. So, without the public, without the commons, without the the government that has provided a lot of this, it wouldn't exist. Um and this was a Senate office report, by the way. This is not just, you know, Goldman Sachs or you know, different podcasters and stuff that are all spinning the same narrative that, oh no, we haven't seen any jobs disruption. By the way, we have seen jobs disruption. Um anyways, moving on. So, then the idea is that we create a public endowment. The wealth is created from public resources and societal disruption must elevate the standard of living for all everyday citizens, not just tech founders. So, the idea is that the rising tide should lift all boats, and this is how we do it. So, the mandate that he laid out are debatable. The first one is the most debatable. The second one is is a little bit more um let's just say palatable, depending on how it's framed. So, the first one is democratic control. Government receives voting shares and equal board representation in the named labs. Power to block corporate decisions deemed harmful to citizens. Intent: Prevent a handful of executives from dictating societal transformation behind closed doors. This is extraordinarily dangerous. When people talk about Marxism and central planning and socialism, this is what they are talking about. This is functionally no different from the Chinese Communist Party seizing control of big important companies. So, this one is just completely a bad idea for reasons that anyone who's familiar with history would understand. Our government is very capricious. Uh so, you know, we don't necessarily want um you know, elected politicians. Now, yes, we do want elected politicians to have some say, but them getting unilateral say is also not necessarily good. We have not really had this kind of level of control. So, this makes me think that the legislation was really designed to fail. Now, it's not even a piece of legislation yet. It was an op-ed. So, the opinion piece is designed to fail. Now, however, the other mandate is broad wealth sharing. Dividends distributed as direct cash payments to the American people, long-term funding earmarked for healthcare, education, and housing. Yes, this is very much in line with post-labor economics where number one, you want proceeds from a sovereign wealth fund to go directly to the people, not necessarily as in-kind services, which is what healthcare, education, and housing are. I would also add transportation. You can do both. There are There are wealth funds out there in the world that do both. Some of them provide in-kind services, some of them provide housing subsidies, some of them provide direct payments. Um when you run the numbers, the Norwegian uh sovereign uh sovereign wealth fund provides something uh up to like 20 to 30% of total household compensation, meaning that the amount of income that the that the household has is effectively 20 to 30% higher than their actual nominal income, but they don't get it as an actual cash dividend, they get it as in-kind services, meaning that it's money that they didn't have to spend. So, however you slice it and you know, there's some debate about you know, oh well, if you just give people money, you know, we should give people universal basic capital or universal basic services. And that is basically a fake argument. We should do all of the above. We need to give people money because of the economic concept called double coincidence of wants. Um for instance, you don't need health care all the time. You don't need education all the time. You do need housing all the time and transportation all the time. Um but really what you need is like food all the time. Uh so, because of because of that because free health care and free education are not fungible, they're not necessarily the optimal target. And of course, there are people on the tech right who believe that we should just give people the potential to make money. Even a mild wealth tax often does this. And you see where companies leave California and they go to Texas or they then re-house their data centers in Ireland or whatever. Then next is the actual United States Constitution.
